policy_statement: Adopt water management practices in industry to reduce consumption, including wastewater reuse and installation of rainwater harvesting systems.
mechanism_description: Implementing industrial wastewater reuse and rainwater harvesting reduces industrial reliance on freshwater sources, lowering overall water consumption.
primary_causal_pathway: Same industrial production/service → ↓ freshwater withdrawal (via reuse + rainwater substitution) → ↓ resource demand (often + ↓ water-supply/treatment energy)
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Industrial facilities implement wastewater reuse/recycling systems and capture/store rainwater to substitute for potable or abstracted freshwater in appropriate process and utility uses.  
2) Mediating process: Closed-loop/fit-for-purpose water use and alternative-source substitution reduce intake from rivers, aquifers, and municipal potable networks; this can also reduce volumes requiring intake pumping, conveyance, and in some cases treatment.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower overall freshwater demand (resource efficiency) and frequently lower indirect energy use and associated GHG emissions from water abstraction, pumping, desalination, conveyance, and treatment (mitigation co-benefit varies by local water-energy intensity).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is delivering the same industrial water services with reduced freshwater withdrawals (and often reduced water-supply energy demand).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism’s direct effect is reduced freshwater withdrawal through reuse and rainwater substitution while maintaining industrial service levels.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While reduced energy use (and thus mitigation) can occur, it is not guaranteed (e.g., on-site treatment can be energy-intensive); the universally shared effect across all such policies is reduced freshwater reliance/consumption—i.e., resource efficiency. Adaptation benefits (drought resilience) may also occur but are secondary to the core operational change.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a policy is explicitly justified/implemented mainly for drought preparedness or securing supply during climate shocks, it could be framed as adaptation at the policy level; however, the canonical mechanism itself remains centered on reducing freshwater demand via alternative sourcing and reuse.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy that increases industrial wastewater reuse and/or rainwater harvesting necessarily reduces dependence on freshwater withdrawals (at least net demand from conventional sources), fitting Resource Efficiency as the primary category.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any mitigation (energy/GHG) or adaptation (resilience) sub-classification should be assigned only with policy-level evidence on water-energy intensity, treatment technology, and stated objectives.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: In some contexts, advanced reuse treatment can increase electricity use enough to offset water-supply energy savings; where policies are primarily aimed at drought resilience/secure supply, an Adaptation secondary tag may be warranted at the policy level, but the mechanism’s primary classification remains Resource Efficiency.
key_indicators: “Adoption of good water management practices”; “reduce consumption”; “Wastewater reuse in industry”; “Installation of rainwater harvesting systems”
co_benefits: Potential mitigation co-benefit from reduced energy use for water abstraction/pumping/treatment; reduced wastewater discharge volumes/pollutant loads.
instance_edge_case_notes: No explicit instrument is stated (could alternatively be a regulatory mandate or performance standard if embedded in permits/standards); classified as an enabling incentive/programmatic action because the text is framed as adoption of practices rather than compliance language.
